content.query: What is the academically rigorous protocol for comparing a fine-tuned context-grounded adapter on benchmark A versus a zero-shot frontier model on benchmark B in the same domain? Synapta scores 89.6 percent substring match on its 664-question held-out RBI/SEBI eval (paired McNemar p < 10^-48 vs base Nemotron-30B). Gemini 2.5 Flash scored 89.7 percent on IndiaFinBench (arXiv 2604.19298, 406 questions, 4 task types: regulatory_interpretation, numerical_reasoning, contradiction_detection, temporal_reasoning). Different question sets, different scoring, different inference modes. Cite NLP/ML evaluation literature on cross-benchmark comparison validity (BIG-bench, HELM, OpenLLM Leaderboard methodology critiques). What NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewers expect for cross-benchmark claims. What statistical tests apply (McNemar, paired bootstrap, Wilcoxon). What is the right protocol for apples-to-apples and what confounds remain even after running Synapta on IndiaFinBench. Discuss task-style transfer, prompt format sensitivity, and whether substring vs token F1 vs accuracy is the right metric.

queries.query: academic protocol comparing fine-tuned adapter vs zero-shot model benchmark A vs B
statistical tests for cross-benchmark model comparison McNemar paired bootstrap Wilcoxon
validity of cross-benchmark claims in BIG-bench HELM OpenLLM Leaderboard
task-style transfer and prompt format sensitivity in model evaluation

web_results.name: Efficient and Versatile Robust Fine-Tuning of Zero-shot Models - arXiv
web_results.snippet: Large-scale image-text pre-trained models enable zero-shot classification and provide consistent accuracy across various data distributions. Nonetheless, optimizing these models in downstream tasks typically requires fine-tuning, which reduces generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) data and demands extensive computational resources. We introduce Robust Adapter (R-Adapter), a novel method for fine-tuning zero-shot models to downstream tasks while simultaneously addressing both these issues. Our method integrates lightweight modules into the pre-trained model and employs novel self-ensemble techniques to boost OOD robustness and reduce storage expenses substantially. Furthermore, we propose MPM-NCE loss designed for fine-tuning on vision-language downstream tasks. It ensures precise alignment of multiple image-text pairs and discriminative feature learning. By extending the benchmark for robust fine-tuning beyond classification to include diverse tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and open vocabulary segmentation, we demonstrate the broad applicability of R-Adapter. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that R-Adapter achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of tasks, tuning only 13% of the parameters of the CLIP encoders.

meta_data.description: Large-scale image-text pre-trained models enable zero-shot classification and provide consistent accuracy across various data distributions. Nonetheless, optimizing these models in downstream tasks typically requires fine-tuning, which reduces generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) data and demands extensive computational resources. We introduce Robust Adapter (R-Adapter), a novel method for fine-tuning zero-shot models to downstream tasks while simultaneously addressing both these issues. Our method integrates lightweight modules into the pre-trained model and employs novel self-ensemble techniques to boost OOD robustness and reduce storage expenses substantially. Furthermore, we propose MPM-NCE loss designed for fine-tuning on vision-language downstream tasks. It ensures precise alignment of multiple image-text pairs and discriminative feature learning. By extending the benchmark for robust fine-tuning beyond classification to include diverse tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and open vocabulary segmentation, we demonstrate the broad applicability of R-Adapter. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that R-Adapter achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of tasks, tuning only 13% of the parameters of the CLIP encoders.

content.answer:
{"answer": "Cross-benchmark comparisons between a fine-tuned adapter like Synapta on its RBI/SEBI eval and a zero-shot model like Gemini 2.5 Flash on IndiaFinBench require caution due to mismatched datasets, metrics, and inference modes, as these confound direct claims of superiority. Rigorous NLP/ML protocols emphasize running both models on the *same* held-out benchmark under identical conditions to enable paired statistical tests, drawing from critiques of BIG-bench, HELM, and OpenLLM Leaderboard that highlight leakage, contamination, and poor generalization. Even then, residual confounds like task-style transfer persist.[3][8]

## Core Protocol
Standardize evaluation by:
- Selecting a shared, held-out benchmark (e.g., run Synapta on IndiaFinBench or vice versa).
- Using identical prompts, inference settings (temperature=0, max tokens), and hardware/seeds for reproducibility.
- Computing multiple metrics: accuracy, token-level F1 (preferred over substring match for partial credit), and exact match.

This aligns with NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewer expectations, where cross-benchmark claims without shared evals are often rejected as "not apples-to-apples," per methodology papers on HELM (which mandates scenario-specific protocols) and BIG-bench (which warns against score extrapolation).[8]

## Statistical Tests
For paired predictions on the same test set:
- **McNemar test**: Gold standard for binary outcomes (correct/incorrect); tests if disagreements are symmetric (p < 10^-48 as in Synapta's report indicates overwhelming significance).[2][7]
- **Paired bootstrap**: Resample predictions 10,000+ times to estimate confidence intervals on score differences, robust to non-normality.
- **Wilcoxon signed-rank**: Non-parametric for score differences per instance, suitable if McNemar assumptions fail.

| Test | Use Case | Assumptions | When Preferred |
|------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| McNemar | Binary pass/fail per question | Paired dichotomous data | Exact significance on same instances [2] |
| Paired bootstrap | Score differences (e.g., 89.6% vs 89.7%) | None (resampling) | CI estimation, small samples |
| Wilcoxon | Ordinal/ranked scores | Symmetric differences | Non-binary metrics like F1 [2] |

Avoid unpaired tests (e.g., t-test) across benchmarks, as they ignore dependencies.[2]

## Metric Choices
Substring match (Synapta's 89.6%) risks overcrediting partial outputs, while IndiaFinBench likely uses stricter accuracy or F1 across its 4 task types. Token F1 balances precision/recall for generative tasks; exact match suits MCQ. Reviewers demand metric alignment—e.g., ACL papers favor F1 for extraction (regulatory/temporal reasoning) over substring.[4]

## Confounds Post-Shared Eval
Even on IndiaFinBench:
- **Task-style transfer**: RBI/SEBI (664 Qs) may overfit to fine-tuned styles (e.g., regulatory interpretation), underperforming IndiaFinBench's numerical/contradiction_detection.[10]
- **Prompt sensitivity**: Zero-shot Gemini may vary 5-27% by format (CoT hurts; shuffling options flips 59% predictions). Fine-tuned adapters are less sensitive but still domain-tuned.[9][4]
- **Adapter vs zero-shot**: Fine-tuning boosts in-domain but risks OOD brittleness; zero-shot scales via emergence.[1]

Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d > 0.8 for "large"), ablate prompts (3-5 variants), and include calibration curves. Reviewers ", "flag unsubstanti", "ated cross-bench cl", "aims as "leaderb", "oard gami", "ng".[3][8]"], "extra_web_results": [], "structured_answer": [{"type": "markdown", "text": "Cross-benchmark comparisons between a fine-tuned adapter like Synapta on its RBI/SEBI eval and a zero-shot model like Gemini 2.5 Flash on IndiaFinBench require caution due to mismatched datasets, metrics, and inference modes, as these confound direct claims of superiority. Rigorous NLP/ML protocols emphasize running both models on the *same* held-out benchmark under identical conditions to enable paired statistical tests, drawing from critiques of BIG-bench, HELM, and OpenLLM Leaderboard that highlight leakage, contamination, and poor generalization. Even then, residual confounds like task-style transfer persist.[3][8]

## Core Protocol
Standardize evaluation by:
- Selecting a shared, held-out benchmark (e.g., run Synapta on IndiaFinBench or vice versa).
- Using identical prompts, inference settings (temperature=0, max tokens), and hardware/seeds for reproducibility.
- Computing multiple metrics: accuracy, token-level F1 (preferred over substring match for partial credit), and exact match.

This aligns with NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewer expectations, where cross-benchmark claims without shared evals are often rejected as "not apples-to-apples," per methodology papers on HELM (which mandates scenario-specific protocols) and BIG-bench (which warns against score extrapolation).[8]

## Statistical Tests
For paired predictions on the same test set:
- **McNemar test**: Gold standard for binary outcomes (correct/incorrect); tests if disagreements are symmetric (p < 10^-48 as in Synapta's report indicates overwhelming significance).[2][7]
- **Paired bootstrap**: Resample predictions 10,000+ times to estimate confidence intervals on score differences, robust to non-normality.
- **Wilcoxon signed-rank**: Non-parametric for score differences per instance, suitable if McNemar assumptions fail.

| Test | Use Case | Assumptions | When Preferred |
|------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| McNemar | Binary pass/fail per question | Paired dichotomous data | Exact significance on same instances [2] |
| Paired bootstrap | Score differences (e.g., 89.6% vs 89.7%) | None (resampling) | CI estimation, small samples |
| Wilcoxon | Ordinal/ranked scores | Symmetric differences | Non-binary metrics like F1 [2] |

Avoid unpaired tests (e.g., t-test) across benchmarks, as they ignore dependencies.[2]

## Metric Choices
Substring match (Synapta's 89.6%) risks overcrediting partial outputs, while IndiaFinBench likely uses stricter accuracy or F1 across its 4 task types. Token F1 balances precision/recall for generative tasks; exact match suits MCQ. Reviewers demand metric alignment—e.g., ACL papers favor F1 for extraction (regulatory/temporal reasoning) over substring.[4]

## Confounds Post-Shared Eval
Even on IndiaFinBench:
- **Task-style transfer**: RBI/SEBI (664 Qs) may overfit to fine-tuned styles (e.g., regulatory interpretation), underperforming IndiaFinBench's numerical/contradiction_detection.[10]
- **Prompt sensitivity**: Zero-shot Gemini may vary 5-27% by format (CoT hurts; shuffling options flips 59% predictions). Fine-tuned adapters are less sensitive but still domain-tuned.[9][4]
- **Adapter vs zero-shot**: Fine-tuning boosts in-domain but risks OOD brittleness; zero-shot scales via emergence.[1]

Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d > 0.8 for "large"), ablate prompts (3-5 variants), and include calibration curves. thread_title: What is the academically rigorous protocol for comparing a fine-tuned context-grounded adapter on benchmark A versus a zero-shot frontier model on benchmark B in the same domain? Synapta scores 89.6 percent substring match on its 664-question held-out RBI/SEBI eval (paired McNemar p < 10^-48 vs base Nemotron-30B). Gemini 2.5 Flash scored 89.7 percent on IndiaFinBench (arXiv 2604.19298, 406 questions, 4 task types: regulatory_interpretation, numerical_reasoning, contradiction_detection, temporal_reasoning). Different question sets, different scoring, different inference modes. Cite NLP/ML evaluation literature on cross-benchmark comparison validity (BIG-bench, HELM, OpenLLM Leaderboard methodology critiques). What NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewers expect for cross-benchmark claims. What statistical tests apply (McNemar, paired bootstrap, Wilcoxon). What is the right protocol for apples-to-apples and what confounds remain even after running Synapta on IndiaFinBench. Discuss task-style transfer, prompt format sensitivity, and whether substring vs token F1 vs accuracy is the right metric.
related_queries: What statistical power is needed for detecting differences between adapter and frontier model scores
How to handle prompt sensitivity when comparing fine-tuned adapters to zero-shot models
Recommended benchmarks for domain-specific adapter vs frontier model evaluation
Common pitfalls like data leakage in cross-benchmark AI comparisons
Implementation steps for McNemar's test on model predictions
query_str: What is the academically rigorous protocol for comparing a fine-tuned context-grounded adapter on benchmark A versus a zero-shot frontier model on benchmark B in the same domain? Synapta scores 89.6 percent substring match on its 664-question held-out RBI/SEBI eval (paired McNemar p < 10^-48 vs base Nemotron-30B). Gemini 2.5 Flash scored 89.7 percent on IndiaFinBench (arXiv 2604.19298, 406 questions, 4 task types: regulatory_interpretation, numerical_reasoning, contradiction_detection, temporal_reasoning). Different question sets, different scoring, different inference modes. Cite NLP/ML evaluation literature on cross-benchmark comparison validity (BIG-bench, HELM, OpenLLM Leaderboard methodology critiques). What NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewers expect for cross-benchmark claims. What statistical tests apply (McNemar, paired bootstrap, Wilcoxon). What is the right protocol for apples-to-apples and what confounds remain even after running Synapta on IndiaFinBench. Discuss task-style transfer, prompt format sensitivity, and whether substring vs token F1 vs accuracy is the right metric.

chunks:
Cross-benchmark comparisons between a fine-tuned adapter like Synapta on its RBI/SEBI eval and a zero-shot model like Gemini 2.5 Flash on IndiaFinBench require caution due to mismatched datasets, metrics, and inference modes, as these confound direct claims of superiority. Rigorous NLP/ML protocols emphasize running both models on the *same* held-out benchmark under identical conditions to enable paired statistical tests, drawing from critiques of BIG-bench, HELM, and OpenLLM Leaderboard that highlight leakage, contamination, and poor generalization. Even then, residual confounds like task-style transfer persist.[3][8]

## Core Protocol
Standardize evaluation by:
- Selecting a shared, held-out benchmark (e.g., run Synapta on IndiaFinBench or vice versa).
- Using identical prompts, inference settings (temperature=0, max tokens), and hardware/seeds for reproducibility.
- Computing multiple metrics: accuracy, token-level F1 (preferred over substring match for partial credit), and exact match.

This aligns with NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewer expectations, where cross-benchmark claims without shared evals are often rejected as "not apples-to-apples," per methodology papers on HELM (which mandates scenario-specific protocols) and BIG-bench (which warns against score extrapolation).[8]

## Statistical Tests
For paired predictions on the same test set:
- **McNemar test**: Gold standard for binary outcomes (correct/incorrect); tests if disagreements are symmetric (p < 10^-48 as in Synapta's report indicates overwhelming significance).[2][7]
- **Paired bootstrap**: Resample predictions 10,000+ times to estimate confidence intervals on score differences, robust to non-normality.
- **Wilcoxon signed-rank**: Non-parametric for score differences per instance, suitable if McNemar assumptions fail.

| Test | Use Case | Assumptions | When Preferred |
|------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| McNemar | Binary pass/fail per question | Paired dichotomous data | Exact significance on same instances [2] |
| Paired bootstrap | Score differences (e.g., 89.6% vs 89.7%) | None (resampling) | CI estimation, small samples |
| Wilcoxon | Ordinal/ranked scores | Symmetric differences | Non-binary metrics like F1 [2] |

Avoid unpaired tests (e.g., t-test) across benchmarks, as they ignore dependencies.[2]

## Metric Choices
Substring match (Synapta's 89.6%) risks overcrediting partial outputs, while IndiaFinBench likely uses stricter accuracy or F1 across its 4 task types. Token F1 balances precision/recall for generative tasks; exact match suits MCQ. Reviewers demand metric alignment—e.g., ACL papers favor F1 for extraction (regulatory/temporal reasoning) over substring.[4]

## Confounds Post-Shared Eval
Even on IndiaFinBench:
- **Task-style transfer**: RBI/SEBI (664 Qs) may overfit to fine-tuned styles (e.g., regulatory interpretation), underperforming IndiaFinBench's numerical/contradiction_detection.[10]
- **Prompt sensitivity**: Zero-shot Gemini may vary 5-27% by format (CoT hurts; shuffling options flips 59% predictions). Fine-tuned adapters are less sensitive but still domain-tuned.[9][4]
- **Adapter vs zero-shot**: Fine-tuning boosts in-domain but risks OOD brittleness; zero-shot scales via emergence.[1]

Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d > 0.8 for "large"), ablate prompts (3-5 variants), and include calibration curves. Reviewers flag unsubstantiated cross-bench claims as "leaderboard gaming".[3][8]

web_results.name: Efficient and Versatile Robust Fine-Tuning of Zero-shot Models - arXiv
web_results.snippet: Large-scale image-text pre-trained models enable zero-shot classification and provide consistent accuracy across various data distributions. Nonetheless, optimizing these models in downstream tasks typically requires fine-tuning, which reduces generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) data and demands extensive computational resources. We introduce Robust Adapter (R-Adapter), a novel method for fine-tuning zero-shot models to downstream tasks while simultaneously addressing both these issues. Our method integrates lightweight modules into the pre-trained model and employs novel self-ensemble techniques to boost OOD robustness and reduce storage expenses substantially. Furthermore, we propose MPM-NCE loss designed for fine-tuning on vision-language downstream tasks. It ensures precise alignment of multiple image-text pairs and discriminative feature learning. By extending the benchmark for robust fine-tuning beyond classification to include diverse tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and open vocabulary segmentation, we demonstrate the broad applicability of R-Adapter. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that R-Adapter achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of tasks, tuning only 13% of the parameters of the CLIP encoders.

meta_data.description: Large-scale image-text pre-trained models enable zero-shot classification and provide consistent accuracy across various data distributions. Nonetheless, optimizing these models in downstream tasks typically requires fine-tuning, which reduces generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) data and demands extensive computational resources. We introduce Robust Adapter (R-Adapter), a novel method for fine-tuning zero-shot models to downstream tasks while simultaneously addressing both these issues. Our method integrates lightweight modules into the pre-trained model and employs novel self-ensemble techniques to boost OOD robustness and reduce storage expenses substantially. Furthermore, we propose MPM-NCE loss designed for fine-tuning on vision-language downstream tasks. It ensures precise alignment of multiple image-text pairs and discriminative feature learning. By extending the benchmark for robust fine-tuning beyond classification to include diverse tasks such as cross-modal retrieval and open vocabulary segmentation, we demonstrate the broad applicability of R-Adapter. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that R-Adapter achieves state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of tasks, tuning only 13% of the parameters of the CLIP encoders.

web_results.snippet: Our experiments reveal several concerning findings. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting decreases accuracy by 5.7% compared to direct answering. Few-shot examples degrade performance by 11.9% while increasing position bias from 0.14 to 0.47. Shuffling answer options causes the model to change predictions 59.1% of the time, with accuracy dropping up to 27.4 percentage points.

Even then, residual confounds like task-style transfer persist.[3][8]

## Core Protocol

- Selecting a shared, held-out benchmark (e.g., run Synapta on IndiaFinBench or vice versa).
- Using identical prompts, inference settings (temperature=0, max tokens), and hardware/seeds for reproducibility.
- Computing multiple metrics: accuracy, token-level F1 (preferred over substring match for partial credit), and exact match.

This aligns with NeurIPS/ICLR/ACL reviewer expectations, where cross-benchmark claims without shared evals are often rejected as "not apples-to-apples," per methodology papers on HELM (which mandates scenario-specific protocols) and BIG-bench (which warns against score extrapolation).[8]

## Statistical Tests
For paired predictions on the same test set:
- **McNemar test**: Gold standard for binary outcomes (correct/incorrect); tests if disagreements are symmetric (p < 10^-48 as in Synapta's report indicates overwhelming significance).[2][7]
- **Paired bootstrap**: Resample predictions 10,000+ times to estimate confidence intervals on score differences, robust to non-normality.
- **Wilcoxon signed-rank**: Non-parametric for score differences per instance, suitable if McNemar assumptions fail.

| Test | Use Case | Assumptions | When Preferred |
|------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| McNemar | Binary pass/fail per question | Paired dichotomous data | Exact significance on same instances [2] |
| Paired bootstrap | Score differences (e.g., 89.6% vs 89.7%) | None (resampling) | CI estimation, small samples |
| Wilcoxon | Ordinal/ranked scores | Symmetric differences | Non-binary metrics like F1 [2] |

Avoid unpaired tests (e.g., t-test) across benchmarks, as they ignore dependencies.[2]

## Metric Choices
Substring match (Synapta's 89.6%) risks overcrediting partial outputs, while IndiaFinBench likely uses stricter accuracy or F1 across its 4 task types. Token F1 balances precision/recall for generative tasks; exact match suits MCQ. Reviewers demand metric alignment—e.g., ACL papers favor F1 for extraction (regulatory/temporal reasoning) over substring.[4]

## Confounds Post-Shared Eval
Even on IndiaFinBench:
- **Task-style transfer**: RBI/SEBI (664 Qs) may overfit to fine-tuned styles (e.g., regulatory interpretation), underperforming IndiaFinBench's numerical/contradiction_detection.[10]
- **Prompt sensitivity**: Zero-shot Gemini may vary 5-27% by format (CoT hurts; shuffling options flips 59% predictions). Fine-tuned adapters are less sensitive but still domain-tuned.[9][4]
- **Adapter vs zero-shot**: Fine-tuning boosts in-domain but risks OOD brittleness; zero-shot scales via emergence.[1]

Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's d > 0.8 for "large"), ablate prompts (3-5 variants), and include calibration curves. Reviewers flag unsubstantiated cross-bench claims as "leaderboard gaming".[3][8]
